subUnits.title: 6.1 Meaning and Types of Renewable and Non-Renewable Resources
notes: • Resources that can be replenished naturally over short periods relative to human lifetimes
• Can be used sustainably if managed properly
• Uses: Electricity (solar panels), heating, drying, agriculture
• Uses: Electricity generation via wind turbines
• Advantages: Clean, no fuel cost, scalable
• Limitations: Variable wind speeds, visual/noise impacts, bird hazards
• Energy from flowing or falling water
• Uses: Electricity (dams, run-of-river), irrigation, flood control
• Advantages: Reliable, low operating cost, multi-purpose
• Limitations: High initial cost, environmental/social impacts of dams
• Organic material from plants and animals
• Uses: Fuel (firewood, charcoal, biogas), electricity, biofuels
• Advantages: Widely available, can use waste, carbon-neutral if managed
• Limitations: Land competition with food, air pollution from burning
• Advantages: Reliable, low emissions, small footprint
• Renewable through hydrological cycle but can be depleted locally
• Uses: Timber, fuelwood, medicine, biodiversity, climate regulation
• Resources that form over geological time scales (millions of years) and cannot be replenished within human timeframes
• Finite in quantity; extraction depletes stock
• Formed from ancient organic matter under heat and pressure
• Problems: Greenhouse gas emissions, air pollution, finite supply
• Iron, copper, gold, tin, bauxite, etc.
• Problems: Radioactive waste, safety concerns, proliferation risks
• Renewability: Renewable can replenish; non-renewable cannot
• Time scale: Renewable replenishes in years/decades; non-renewable takes millions of years
• Environmental impact: Renewables generally cleaner; fossil fuels major polluters
• Cost structure: Renewables have high upfront, low operating costs; fossil fuels opposite
• Sustainability: Renewables can support long-term development; non-renewables require transition planning

subUnits.youtubeQuery: Renewable non-renewable resources geography WAEC JAMB site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Renewable resources are those that:
opts: Can be replenished naturally over short periods
Are only found in developed countries
quiz.explanation: Renewable resources replenish naturally within human-relevant timeframes (years to decades) if managed sustainably.

quiz.q: Which is a non-renewable resource?
quiz.explanation: Crude oil forms over millions of years from ancient organic matter and cannot be replenished within human lifetimes.

quiz.q: Hydropower is considered renewable because:
opts: Water is replenished through the hydrological cycle
quiz.explanation: The water cycle (evaporation, precipitation, runoff) continuously replenishes water resources, making hydropower renewable.

quiz.q: A major limitation of solar energy is:
quiz.explanation: Solar energy generation depends on sunlight, so it varies with time of day, season, and weather conditions.

quiz.q: Biomass energy is renewable if:
opts: Harvesting rate does not exceed regrowth rate
It is only used for electricity
quiz.explanation: Biomass is sustainable when harvesting (e.g., trees for fuelwood) is balanced by replanting and natural regrowth.

quiz.q: Fossil fuels are non-renewable because they:
opts: Form over millions of years and cannot be replenished quickly
quiz.explanation: Coal, oil, and gas take geological time scales to form; current consumption far exceeds natural formation rates.

quiz.q: Which renewable resource is most suitable for Northern Nigeria?
quiz.explanation: Northern Nigeria has high solar insolation (sunshine hours), making solar energy particularly viable for electricity and heating.

quiz.q: A key advantage of renewable resources is:
opts: They generally have lower environmental impacts
quiz.explanation: Most renewables produce little to no greenhouse gases or air pollutants during operation, reducing environmental harm.

quiz.q: Metallic minerals like iron and copper are non-renewable because:
opts: They form over geological time and extraction depletes finite deposits
They are only used in construction
quiz.explanation: Mineral deposits form through slow geological processes; mining extracts finite stocks that cannot be quickly replenished.

quiz.q: Sustainable resource management requires:
opts: Balancing use with conservation and considering renewability
quiz.explanation: Sustainability involves using resources at rates that allow renewal (for renewables) and planning transitions (for non-renewables).

subUnits.title: 6.2 Advantages and Disadvantages of Renewable and Non-Renewable Resources
notes: • Low or zero greenhouse gas emissions during operation
• Reduced air and water pollution compared to fossil fuels
• Less habitat destruction when properly sited
• Domestic resources reduce dependence on imported fuels
• Diversifies energy mix, reducing vulnerability to price shocks
• Distributed generation (e.g., rooftop solar) enhances resilience
• Job creation in manufacturing, installation, maintenance
• Improved health from reduced air pollution
• Community ownership models empower local people
• Can support long-term development without depletion
• Solar and wind depend on weather and time of day
• Grid integration challenges for variable sources
• Longer payback periods than some alternatives
• Large land area needed for solar/wind farms
• Potential competition with agriculture or conservation
• Material requirements (e.g., rare earths for turbines)
• Need for grid upgrades and smart technologies
• Limited local technical capacity for maintenance
• Storage technology still developing and costly
• Best resources often in remote areas (e.g., windy coasts, sunny deserts)
• Transmission losses and costs to deliver to demand centers
• Fossil fuels contain concentrated energy per unit mass/volume
• Enables compact, powerful engines for transport and industry
• Established infrastructure for extraction, transport, use
• Existing investments in extraction, refining, distribution
• Fossil fuels used for electricity, transport, heating, petrochemicals
• Minerals essential for construction, manufacturing, technology
• Often cheaper upfront than renewables in many contexts
• Subsidies and externalities not fully priced in
• Greenhouse gas emissions driving climate change
• Water contamination from mining, drilling, spills
• Land degradation from extraction and waste
• Finite stocks will eventually run out
• Extraction becomes harder and costlier as easy reserves deplete
• Price volatility due to market speculation, geopolitics
• Import dependence creates balance of payments pressures
• Stranded asset risks as world transitions to clean energy
• Air pollution causes millions of premature deaths annually
• Occupational hazards in mining and extraction
• Community conflicts over land and resource rights
• Intergenerational inequity: using up resources for future generations
• Lock-in to high-carbon infrastructure delays transition
• Renewable potential: High solar in North, hydro on Niger/Benue, biomass from agriculture
• Non-renewable dominance: Oil/gas drive economy but cause environmental and social issues
• Transition challenges: Need for investment, skills, policy frameworks for renewables
• Opportunities: Leapfrog to distributed renewables, create green jobs, improve energy access

subUnits.youtubeQuery: Advantages disadvantages renewable non-renewable resources geography site:youtube.com

quiz.q: A major environmental advantage of renewable resources is:
opts: Low or zero greenhouse gas emissions during operation
quiz.explanation: Most renewables produce minimal greenhouse gases during operation, helping mitigate climate change compared to fossil fuels.

quiz.q: A key disadvantage of solar and wind energy is:
opts: Intermittency and variability requiring storage or backup
They are only for rich countries
quiz.explanation: Solar and wind depend on weather and time, so they need storage or complementary sources for reliable power supply.

quiz.q: Non-renewable resources are advantageous because they:
opts: Provide reliable, on-demand energy with existing infrastructure
quiz.explanation: Fossil fuels and minerals offer high energy density and dispatchability, with mature infrastructure for extraction and use.

quiz.q: A major disadvantage of fossil fuels is:
opts: Greenhouse gas emissions driving climate change
quiz.explanation: Burning fossil fuels releases CO₂ and other pollutants, contributing significantly to global warming and health problems.

quiz.q: High initial costs for renewables mean that:
opts: Significant upfront investment is needed, but operating costs are low
quiz.explanation: While solar/wind require substantial capital for installation, their fuel is free and maintenance costs are relatively low.

quiz.q: Resource depletion is a problem for non-renewables because:
opts: Finite stocks will eventually run out, making extraction harder
They are only used in agriculture
quiz.explanation: Non-renewable resources exist in finite quantities; as easy reserves deplete, extraction becomes costlier and more environmentally damaging.

quiz.q: Energy security is enhanced by renewables because they:
opts: Use domestic resources, reducing reliance on imported fuels
quiz.explanation: Harnessing local sun, wind, water, or biomass reduces vulnerability to global fuel price shocks and supply disruptions.

quiz.q: A social benefit of renewable energy is:
opts: Improved health from reduced pollution and energy access for remote areas
quiz.explanation: Cleaner energy improves public health, and decentralized renewables can electrify remote communities, enabling development.

quiz.q: The intermittency of renewables can be addressed by:
opts: Energy storage, grid upgrades, and hybrid systems
quiz.explanation: Batteries, pumped hydro, demand management, and combining sources (e.g., solar + wind + hydro) can balance variable supply.

quiz.q: Sustainable development requires transitioning from non-renewables to renewables because:
opts: Non-renewables are finite and polluting, while renewables can support long-term well-being
quiz.explanation: Renewables offer a path to meet current needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs, aligning with sustainability principles.
